topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The text message glowed on my screen, the words stark and final. "Who are you?" It was from Liam. I reread it three times, my thumb hovering over the delete button before slamming my palm against the desk. A slow, hot flush crept up my neck, tightening my chest until breathing felt shallow. How could he just… erase me? All those scraped knees, the shared secrets under the old oak tree, the inside jokes that formed the very bedrock of my world—gone? I stood up, pacing the small apartment, the floorboards groaning under my frantic energy. I needed to see him, to demand an explanation, to force some recognition from that familiar, infuriatingly oblivious face. When he finally answered, his voice was flat, devoid of the warmth I remembered. The sheer emptiness in his tone made my jaw clench so hard I thought my teeth might crack.

[story 2]

I watched him laugh, a genuine, easy sound, and it felt like a physical blow. He was talking about a trip to the coast, a place we’d dreamed about for years, and he didn't even glance over. When I finally managed to interject, my voice was sharp, cutting through the easy banter. "Liam, wait. Remember that summer? The time we tried to build that ridiculous raft?" He blinked, a flicker of confusion crossing his eyes, quickly replaced by blankness. He looked at me as if I were a stranger, a misplaced piece of furniture. The air in the room seemed to thicken, pressing down on my lungs. I gripped my coffee mug until my knuckles turned white, the ceramic digging painfully into my skin. I couldn't believe the casual dismissal. I took a step back, my gaze burning into him, demanding that he finally see the ghost standing right in front of him.

[story 3]

The silence stretched between us, taut and brittle, punctuated only by the distant hum of traffic outside. I felt a sudden, dizzying wave of disbelief wash over me, followed immediately by a burning resentment. I had spent years cultivating a deep, unbreakable bond with Mark. Now, standing here, watching him regard me with such detached curiosity, all that history felt like a cruel, elaborate fabrication. "You don't remember me?" The question came out low, laced with a tremor of disbelief that I fought desperately to mask. Mark shifted uncomfortably, running a hand through his hair as if trying to physically shake off the accusation. His expression was one of genuine bewilderment, the kind reserved for someone who genuinely doesn't grasp the context. I watched him, my fists clenched at my sides, the heat rising in my face until I felt I might combust. I needed him to grasp the magnitude of what he was dismissing.
